does: GuardNet develops a guardrail system using an ensemble of shallow BiLSTM neural networks with approximately 47 million parameters to detect prompt injection and jailbreak attacks on large language models. The core contribution is demonstrating that adversarial robustness in detection relies more on diversity of training examples and calibration of decision thresholds than on model scale. The system achieves competitive detection performance with low latency, reaching AUROC of 0.747 on a blind jailbreak benchmark (n=200) and F1 score of 0.92 on proprietary data (n=50), while explicitly addressing the problem of benchmark contamination and information leakage that can artificially inflate performance estimates.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature addressing LLM safety vulnerabilities, extending prior research on adversarial attack detection by challenging the assumption that larger models necessarily provide better defenses. It combines ensemble learning approaches established in adversarial robustness with lightweight detection architectures, responding to recent work documenting prompt injection and jailbreak attacks as serious threats to deployed LLM systems. The emphasis on threshold calibration and example diversity connects to classical work in ensemble methods and adversarial machine learning while addressing specific evaluation concerns highlighted by recent studies on benchmark contamination in AI safety assessments.
enables: This approach opens pathways for developing resource-efficient safety systems deployable in production environments where low latency and computational efficiency are critical constraints. The finding that ensemble diversity and calibration matter more than scale suggests future work designing targeted ensemble strategies for specific attack types and developing better methods for constructing diverse yet complementary detection modules. It enables investigation into how adversarial robustness mechanisms transfer across different LLM architectures and prompts further research into calibration techniques, cost-effective detection pipelines, and mechanisms for evaluating guardrails on truly held-out data without contamination risks.
